title: SEGMENT 15 — Analytical methods — measuring the glycan

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — Analytical methods — measuring the glycan
You cannot engineer what you cannot measure.
And the entire modern field of glycoengineering depends, foundationally, on a set of analytical methods that have become exquisitely capable over the last two decades.
Let me give you a tour.

The dominant analytical technique for IgG glycan characterization today is mass spectrometry, almost always coupled with some form of liquid chromatography — so the workhorse method is LC-MS or HPLC-MS.

You take your antibody preparation, you cleave the N-glycan off the protein using an enzyme called PNGase F, you label the released glycans with a fluorescent or charge-bearing tag (commonly 2-aminobenzamide, 2-AB, for fluorescence, or procainamide for charge), you separate them by chromatography — often hydrophilic interaction chromatography, HILIC — and you detect them by both fluorescence and mass spectrometry simultaneously.
The chromatography separates glycoforms by their physical properties.
The fluorescence quantifies the relative abundance of each peak.
The mass spectrometry identifies the structure of each peak by molecular weight (with isotope patterns and fragmentation patterns to disambiguate isomers).
The output is a quantitative glycoform profile — typically a table showing the relative percentages of G0F, G1F, G2F, G0, G1, G2, and the various sialylated and bisected variants.
For a Potelligent-produced antibody, you'd expect the profile to be dominated by G0, G1, G2 — the non-fucosylated species — with G0F, G1F, G2F at single-digit percentages or below.
For a conventional CHO-produced antibody, the inverse: G0F, G1F, G2F dominant, the unfucosylated species at maybe 5-10%.
The analytical method has the resolution to distinguish these populations with high confidence and to monitor them across batches.

Higher-resolution analytics include glycopeptide mapping, in which the antibody is digested with a protease (typically trypsin) to generate peptide fragments, and the Asn297-containing peptide is analyzed by LC-MS with its glycan still attached.

This gives you site-specific glycan profiling — useful for Fc fusions where there may be glycans at multiple sites, or for antibodies with Fab glycans in addition to the Fc glycan, where you need to distinguish them.
Released-glycan MALDI-MS is another approach: release the glycans, optionally permethylate them (which improves ionization), and shoot them through a MALDI-TOF mass spectrometer.
This gives a fast mass spectrum of the glycan pool, useful for rapid characterization but with lower quantitative precision than LC-MS.
Capillary electrophoresis with laser-induced fluorescence (CE-LIF) is a complementary separation method that's particularly good for high-throughput quality control.
Many manufacturers use CE-LIF as a routine release assay for glycan profile.
For tracking the broader Fc functional state, beyond just structural glycan analysis, functional assays matter.
ADCC bioassays — using engineered effector cells (often Jurkat-NFAT-luciferase cells expressing FcγRIIIa) and target cells — give a potency readout that is sensitive to glycoform.
Complement activation assays (CDC) similarly.
Surface plasmon resonance (SPR) measures Fc receptor binding affinities directly, and is the gold standard for quantifying the afucose-induced affinity boost to FcγRIIIa.
The analytical landscape is mature.

A modern process analytical setup can monitor glycoform distribution during cell culture in near-real-time, allowing process engineers to tune feed strategy, temperature, pH, and other parameters to drive the glycoform toward the target.
This is what's called quality by design — designing the manufacturing process to deliver a defined glycoform profile, with deviations triggering corrective action.
For a glycoengineered drug, where glycoform is the active mechanism, QbD is not optional.
It's essential.
If you remember one analytical fact from this segment, remember this: HPLC-MS gives you the glycoform profile at percent-resolution, and that profile is the molecular fingerprint of the drug.
Two preparations with different glycoform profiles are, functionally, different drugs.
